result:
Here is a thing I often think: the world is too full of things. Not just objects—plastic forks, lost socks, single mittens—but of moments, of feelings, of unspoken sentences that pile up in the corners of rooms like dust.

We carry so much invisible inventory. The exact shade of blue in the sky on the afternoon your grandfather died. The smell of rain on hot pavement that you inhaled when you were eight and somehow never forgot. The specific, heavy silence that falls after a door slams in anger. All of it, catalogued. A silent, humming archive behind your eyes.

We think of memory as a recording, but it’s more like a constant, gentle act of creation. You are not just remembering the picnic; you are, right now, weaving it again—the tickle of grass, the warmth of the cheese sandwich, the way your friend’s laugh sounded like water over stones. You are the weaver and the tapestry, both. And the loom is never still.

And what about the stories we never get to tell? The woman on the bus, clutching a bouquet of sad tulips, her face a map of a private country. Where is she going? Who are the tulips for? We’ll never know. Her story passes yours for ten seconds, a ghost ship in the night, and then is gone forever. A universe of such stories blinks in and out of existence every minute, in every city. It is overwhelming, this vast, silent traffic of inner lives.

Sometimes I imagine all the unsaid “I love yous.” Not the withheld ones, the bitter ones, but the simple, everyday ones that don’t get spoken because the moment isn’t grand enough. The “I love you” you think when you see your child concentrating fiercely on tying a shoelace. The “I love you” that floats up when your partner hums off-key in the kitchen. They are silent, buoyant balloons, released and floating up into a stratosphere of unvoiced affection. If we could see them, the sky would be permanently rainbow-colored, thick with them.

And the apologies, too. The micro-apologies for tiny betrayals: for being distracted, for forgetting to buy the milk, for not listening fully. They form a kind of low, sorrowful mist around our ankles. We wade through it.

We are taught to pursue happiness, as if it is a butterfly to be netted and mounted. But I wonder if peace isn’t the more attainable, more precious thing. Happiness is a spike on a graph—brilliant, but unsustainable. Peace is the baseline. It is the quiet hum of the refrigerator at 3 a.m. when the house is asleep. It is the acceptance of the dust, the archive, the silent balloons, the mist. It is looking at the messy, over-full world and not needing to tidy it, but just to see it, and to be a part of its mess.

There is a tree outside my window. It is not a remarkable tree. It is neither the oldest nor the tallest nor the most beautifully shaped. But for twenty years, it has been doing its tree work. It has held nests through storms. It has turned sunlight into leaf. It has shed its canopy in autumn with a kind of magnificent indifference, creating a golden carpet on the ground, only to patiently grow it all back again in spring. It does not wonder if it is a good tree. It does not fret over the meaning of its tree-ness. It just is.

Perhaps that is the secret we keep missing. We are human *beings*, but we spend so much time in human *doings* and human *thinkings* that we forget the permission of the *being*. To be, like the tree, is enough. To be a vessel for this odd collection—the blue sky of grief, the laugh like water, the silent I-love-you balloon—is the task.

The world is too full, yes. But what if that’s not a problem to be solved, but a reality to be cherished? The over-fullness is evidence of life. It is the clutter of existence. The alternative is a sterile, empty room. A silence with no hum.

So I sit here, with my 1000 words almost spent, and I think: I will try, today, to be less of an archivist and more of a witness. To let the moments flow through without the frantic need to label and shelve them. To watch the tulip woman and let her mystery be a gift, not a puzzle. To feel the peace of the unremarkable tree.

And maybe, just maybe, I will say one of the silent “I love yous” out loud. I will give it voice. I will pop one of the balloons and let its color stain the air. I will add, consciously, to the beautiful, unbearable, over-full clutter of this world. A tiny act of contribution to the noise and the silence, to the dust and the memory, to the grand, unmanageable, glorious everything.

That seems like a good use of a life. And of 1000 words.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
